title: How to Perform Real-Time DNS Monitoring in Grafana Cloud

blocks.content:
In this interactive guide you'll learn how to set up and run a DNS check in Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring. You will:

- Create and configure a DNS check for any domain
- Set a valid response code and timeout for accurate failure detection
- Select probe locations for global DNS validation
- Set up alerting so you're notified when resolution fails

### **Why Use DNS Checks?**

- **Real-time DNS monitoring** – Detect resolution failures and latency from multiple global locations before they impact users
- **Security monitoring** – Identify DDoS attacks or DNS poisoning attempts early
- **Global DNS validation** – Test that domains resolve correctly from probe locations around the world

## Section 1: Navigate to Synthetic Monitoring

Let's start by navigating to the Testing & Synthetics section in Grafana Cloud. This is where you create and manage all your synthetic checks.
In the main menu, navigate to **Testing & Synthetics**.
Click **Synthetic Monitoring** to open the Synthetics home page.
If this is your first time using Synthetic Monitoring, click the initialization button. You can skip this step otherwise.
Click **Create new check** to get started.
Select **API Endpoint** as the check type. This is where you'll find HTTP, Ping, DNS, TCP, and Traceroute checks.

## Section 2: Configure the DNS Check

Now let's configure the DNS check. You'll give it a name, set the request type to DNS, and specify the domain you want to monitor.
Enter a **Job Name** for your check, for example **Example:DNS**.

blocks.reftarget: label[title='Validate the DNS records for a given host.']
blocks.content: Set the **Request Type** to **DNS**.
Enter your **Request Target** — the domain name to resolve, for example **grafana.com**.

## Section 3: Optional Parameters

In this section you can set the expected DNS response code, configure a timeout, and add labels to organize your check. These help ensure the check accurately detects failures.
Click **Uptime** to move to the next configuration section.
Optionally set the **Valid Response Code**. Choose **NOERROR** to confirm the DNS query returned a valid response. The check will fail if any other response code is returned.
Optionally set a **Timeout**. If the check takes longer than this to run it will be marked as failed. A value of **10 seconds** is suitable for most DNS checks.
Move to the **Labels** section.
Optionally add **Labels** to help organize and filter your checks.

## Section 4: Select Probe Locations and Frequency

Probes are monitoring agents distributed around the world that send DNS queries to your target. Selecting multiple locations validates that your domain resolves correctly across different regions.
Click **Execution** to move to the probe configuration section.
Select one or more **Probe Locations** to run the DNS check from. Choose locations closest to your users or infrastructure to best reflect their experience.
Optionally adjust the **Probe Frequency**. The default of 1 minute is suitable for most checks.

## Section 5: Configure Alerting

Alerting ensures you're notified as soon as DNS resolution starts failing. Set a threshold that reflects your tolerance for missed resolutions.
Click **Alerting** to move to the alerting configuration section.
Enable the **Probe Failed Executions** alert by clicking the checkbox. For example, alert when 2 of 10 executions fail in the last five minutes.
Optionally click **Test** to validate the DNS check before saving.
Click **Save** to create your DNS check. Grafana Cloud will begin executing it immediately.

## 🎉 Congratulations!

You've successfully set up a DNS check in Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring. You can now:

- View results in the out-of-the-box **Synthetic Monitoring dashboard**, showing uptime, reachability, average latency, and frequency
- Monitor **response latency and resource records by probe location** for deeper visibility into global DNS performance
- Receive **alerts** the moment DNS resolution fails or returns unexpected results

To learn more, explore other check types such as HTTP, Ping, and TCP checks — or set up private probes to monitor internal services not accessible from the public internet.
